overview.body: MASTER unit. Two arrivals close Module 4. First, the coin-flip Destination Gate from Labs 3.x and 4.x retires entirely. A TRIVIA gate replaces it: players answer a TRUE/FALSE question about the city they want to visit, and a wrong answer triggers the same get_redirect detour that the old TAILS branch did. Second, sets arrive as the right tool for tracking unique cities and unique hazards across a game. The trip report grows a TRIVIA section with asked / correct / accuracy stats, and the HAZARDS display uses sorted() on a set to show each unique hazard exactly once.

mentorQuote.quote: The trivia gate is the moment the game stops feeling like a coin-flip simulator and starts feeling like a road trip with friends. Anyone who has driven across Texas knows the deal: someone asks a question about the next stop, you either know it or you get rerouted. We are turning randomness into a small piece of agency. That is what good design feels like: the player earns the next mile.

objectives: Add load_trivia(filename) to function_library.py: parse trivia.txt into a dictionary keyed by city, with each value a list of question/answer dicts
Write trivia_gate(dest, trivia, current, distances) as a lab-local function in lab-4-3.py: ask the player a TRUE/FALSE question about the destination; correct earns it, wrong triggers get_redirect
Replace the coin-flip Destination Gate in the in-game loop with a call to trivia_gate
Build unique_cities = set() and unique_hazards = set() at game start; .add() entries every time a city or hazard is recorded
Replace the post-game 'in' operator uniqueness loop with sorted(unique_cities); replace the .copy().sort() hazard sort with sorted(unique_hazards)
Track trivia_asked and trivia_correct as per-game counters; add a TRIVIA section to the trip report showing asked, correct, and accuracy percentage
overview.keyConceptsText: A set in Python is a collection of unique items with O(1) membership testing. my_set = set() creates an empty set. my_set.add(item) inserts an item (or does nothing if it's already present). len(my_set) gives the unique count. sorted(my_set) returns a new sorted list. Sets are unordered, so iterate them via sorted() for stable display. The trivia data shape introduced this lab is a dictionary keyed by city, with each value a list of dicts: trivia['Austin'] -> [{'question': '...', 'answer': 'TRUE'}, ...]. This combines two patterns from CP1 of Lab 4.1 (dict lookup) and CP1 of Lab 4.2 (list of dicts) into one structure.
overview.textbookReference: Chapter 9 sets section (Programs 9–18 through 9–23) covers set creation, .add(), membership testing, and iteration. sorted() on a set is shown in Program 9–21.
programRequirements: Top-of-file comment block on both files
Docstring on every function, including the lab-local trivia_gate
Four data files loaded at startup: city_distances.txt, city_info.txt, hazards.txt, trivia.txt
Three-loop architecture preserved (main menu, play-again, in-game)
Coin-flip Destination Gate is REPLACED by trivia_gate; no flip_coin() call remains in the gate position
unique_cities and unique_hazards are set objects, NOT lists or in-operator loops
Trip report (both on-screen and trip_report.txt) includes a TRIVIA section with asked, correct, and accuracy
Save format is unchanged from Lab 3.2; trivia stats are session-only and reset on resume

namedConstants.name: MILES_MULTIPLIER
namedConstants.description: Multiplier applied to each D20 roll

namedConstants.name: HAZARD_CHANCE
namedConstants.description: Percent chance per turn of a hazard event

overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-3.py and function_library.py. CodeGrade provides all four data files as fixtures.

dataFiles.format: Shared function library, extended this unit with load_trivia

rows.task: Copy lab-4-2.py forward and add load_trivia to the library
Write the trivia_gate function in lab-4-3.py
Replace the coin-flip Destination Gate with trivia_gate
Build unique_cities as a set; .add() at every visit
Build unique_hazards as a set; .add() in the hazard branch
Replace .copy().sort() and the in-operator loop with sorted(set)
Add trivia_asked/trivia_correct counters and TRIVIA section to trip report
Full play-through + a wrong-trivia path, then upload to CodeGrade

beforeYouBegin.intro: Lab 4.3 is the MASTER unit. About half the work is in the lab file this time: the library gets one new function (load_trivia), but the lab file changes in five places: constants, startup, in-game gate, per-game state, and the trip report. [NEW] tags show what's added. [REPLACED] tags show what swaps out. [RETIRED] tags show what gets deleted entirely.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- IN function_library.py ----
all 4.2 functions                                           [KEEP from 4.2]
load_trivia(filename) -> dict[city] -> list of Q/A dicts    [NEW-CP1]

# ---- IN lab-4-3.py ----

# ---- CONSTANTS ----
DISTANCES_FILE, INFO_FILE, HAZARDS_FILE, REPORT_FILE        [KEEP from 4.2]
TRIVIA_FILE = 'trivia.txt'                                  [NEW-CP1]
MILES_MULTIPLIER, HAZARD_CHANCE                             [KEEP from 4.2]

trivia_gate(dest, trivia, current, distances)               [NEW-CP2]
save_trip_report(...) +trivia_asked +trivia_correct         [REPLACED-CP7]

# ---- main() ----
distances = load_city_distances(...)                        [KEEP from 4.2]
city_info = load_city_info(...)                             [KEEP from 4.2]
hazards   = load_hazards(...)                               [KEEP from 4.2]
trivia    = load_trivia(TRIVIA_FILE)                        [NEW-CP1]

MAIN MENU LOOP                                              [KEEP from 4.2]
  PLAY-AGAIN LOOP                                           [KEEP from 4.2]
    │
    │─ Per-game init:
    │     trivia_asked = 0, trivia_correct = 0           [NEW-CP7]
    │     unique_cities = set(visited_cities)             [NEW-CP4]
    │     unique_hazards = set(hazards_list)              [NEW-CP5]
    │
    IN-GAME LOOP                                            [KEEP from 4.2]
      │
      │─ Coin-flip Destination Gate                    [RETIRED-CP3]
      │─ destination, distance, correct =
      │     trivia_gate(wanted, trivia, ...)             [NEW-CP3]
      │     trivia_asked += 1, trivia_correct += correct
      │
      │─ D20 travel loop
      │     │─ if hazard: handle_hazard            [KEEP from 4.2]
      │     │     hazards_list.append(name)
      │     │     unique_hazards.add(name)            [NEW-CP5]
      │
      │─ Arrival:
      │     visited_cities.append(current_city)
      │     unique_cities.add(current_city)              [NEW-CP4]
      │
      │─ Continue / Save & quit / Quit               [KEEP from 4.2]
    
    Trip report on screen + trip_report.txt:
      ROUTE                                                 [KEEP from 4.2]
      Unique cities = len(unique_cities)                    [REPLACED-CP6]
      HAZARDS = sorted(unique_hazards)                      [REPLACED-CP6]
      STATISTICS                                            [KEEP from 4.2]
      TRIVIA: asked / correct / accuracy                    [NEW-CP7]

content.body: A clean starting point for Lab 4.3. Copy lab-4-2.py to lab-4-3.py, update the docstring, and add one new function to function_library.py: load_trivia(filename). It reads trivia.txt line-by-line and groups questions by city, returning a dictionary where each value is a list of question/answer dicts.

Trivia is the foundation for the new Destination Gate. Loading it once at startup and indexing by city means the gate function in CP2 can ask trivia['Austin'] and immediately get back the question pool for Austin. The grouping happens in the loader, not the gate. This is the same Module 4 pattern you have seen twice now: parse once at startup, index forever after.

content.quote: Look at the three loaders side-by-side after this CP: city_distances returns nested dicts, city_info returns dict-of-dicts, hazards returns a list of dicts, trivia returns a dict-of-lists-of-dicts. Each one matches the access pattern the game needs. Distances need a graph; info needs a table; hazards need random sampling; trivia needs grouping by key. Pick the shape that makes the consumer code easiest to read.

items: Copy lab-4-2.py to lab-4-3.py.
Update the docstring at the top of lab-4-3.py: title becomes Lab 4.3: Trivia Challenge and the description should mention the trivia gate and sets.
Add TRIVIA_FILE = 'trivia.txt' to the constants block alongside the other file constants.
In function_library.py, add a new section header comment block titled TRIVIA FUNCTIONS (Added in Lab 4-3). Place it after the HAZARD FUNCTIONS section.
Define load_trivia(filename) with a full docstring describing the dict-of-lists-of-dicts return shape.
Inside the function, create an empty dictionary called trivia.
Open the file and loop: for line in infile:. Call parts = line.strip().split(',').
Pull out city = parts[0], question = parts[1], answer = parts[2].
If city not in trivia:, initialize trivia[city] = []. Then trivia[city].append({'question': question, 'answer': answer}).
Close the file and return trivia.
In main(), add trivia = function_library.load_trivia(TRIVIA_FILE) after the hazards load. Print a load banner: print(f'Loaded {total_questions} trivia questions across {len(trivia)} cities.')

references.description: Each line builds one Q/A dict, just like a hazard dict in 4.2. The difference is that they're grouped under city keys instead of appended to a flat list.
if key not in d: d[key] = [] followed by d[key].append(value): the canonical 'group items by key' pattern in Python.

content.body: Add a DEBUG block under if __name__ == '__main__': in function_library.py that prints the trivia structure. Delete it before moving on.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
trivia = load_trivia('trivia.txt')
print(f'Cities with trivia: {len(trivia)}')
for city in sorted(trivia):
    print(f'  {city}: {len(trivia[city])} questions')
print('Sample Austin question:', trivia['Austin'][0])

content.output:
Cities with trivia: 10
  Austin: 3 questions
  Corpus Christi: 1 questions
  Dallas: 2 questions
  El Paso: 1 questions
  Fort Worth: 2 questions
  Galveston: 1 questions
  Houston: 4 questions
  Laredo: 1 questions
  San Antonio: 4 questions
  Texarkana: 1 questions
Sample Austin question: {'question': 'Austin is the capital of Texas', 'answer': 'TRUE'}

rows: Forgetting the if city not in trivia: guard
Without it, every line overwrites the previous question for that city. Cities with multiple questions end up holding only the last one.
Storing questions as a flat list instead of grouped by city
Flat lists work, but the gate function in CP2 would then have to scan every entry. The grouped shape makes the lookup O(1).
Hardcoding 'trivia.txt' in the load call
Use the TRIVIA_FILE constant: function_library.load_trivia(TRIVIA_FILE). Constants live at the top of the file for a reason.

items: lab-4-3.py exists and plays identically to 4.2 at this point
TRIVIA_FILE constant is declared in lab-4-3.py
function_library.py has a new TRIVIA FUNCTIONS section with load_trivia inside
Load banner at startup reports 20 questions across 10 cities
DEBUG block is removed before moving on

content.body: trivia_gate(dest, trivia, current, distances) defined in lab-4-3.py: the lab file, not the library. The function looks up trivia for the destination city, picks a random question, prompts the player for TRUE or FALSE, and returns a 3-tuple (final_dest, distance, correct). A correct answer returns the chosen destination; a wrong answer calls function_library.get_redirect to detour the player elsewhere.

Think about which functions belong in the library and which belong in the lab file. The library holds general-purpose helpers: data loaders, dice rolls, input cleaning, things that any future game could reuse. The lab file holds game-specific orchestration. trivia_gate is the second category: it knows the game's TRUE/FALSE answer format, the banners it prints, and the exact way it routes a wrong answer through get_redirect. None of that is reusable for the next game. Putting it in the lab file keeps the boundary clean.

content.quote: This is the heart of the new game feel. The player picks a destination, gets a question, and earns the next leg. Wrong answers are not a punishment; they are a detour, the same way get_redirect rerouted them on a TAILS flip in Module 3. Same mechanical outcome; entirely different feeling at the keyboard. Take care with the messaging on both branches. 'WRONG. The answer was X. Detour to Y instead.' reads as 'oh well, on we go.' That is the tone we want.

items: In lab-4-3.py, define trivia_gate at the top of the file, after the constants but before save_trip_report. Include a full docstring describing the four parameters and the 3-tuple return.
Pick a random question for the destination: q = random.choice(trivia[dest]). You will need import random at the top of the lab file (the library already imports it for itself).
Print the banner: '--- TRIVIA TIME! ---', then f'To reach {dest}, answer this question:', then a blank line, then f' {q["question"]}'.
Read the player's answer: answer = function_library.clean_input('TRUE or FALSE? '). clean_input handles case and whitespace.
Normalize the answer. Accept 'T' or 'TRUE' as TRUE, 'F' or 'FALSE' as FALSE. Anything else counts as wrong: print 'Invalid answer! Counting as wrong.' and set player_answer = 'INVALID'.
Compare: if player_answer == q['answer']: print the correct-answer banner and return (dest, distances[current][dest], True).

Otherwise print the wrong-answer banner including the correct answer, call function_library.get_redirect(current, distances, dest) to pick a detour, print the detour, and return (redirect, redirect_miles, False).

references.description: trivia[dest] is a dictionary lookup; random.choice on the resulting list picks one Q/A dict.
clean_input normalizes the answer to uppercase, so a single check covers true / True / TRUE / True .

content.body: Add a temporary call at the very top of main() (before the main menu loop) to exercise trivia_gate directly. Try answering correctly once, incorrectly once, and with junk input once. Confirm the return tuple matches the expected shape each time. Delete the temporary call before moving on.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
trivia = function_library.load_trivia(TRIVIA_FILE)
distances = function_library.load_city_distances(DISTANCES_FILE)
result = trivia_gate('Austin', trivia, 'San Antonio', distances)
print('Return:', result)

rows: Putting trivia_gate in function_library.py
It does game-specific work (TRUE/FALSE prompts, on-screen banners, redirect orchestration). Library functions stay general-purpose. Lab-local stays lab-local.
Forgetting to import random in the lab file
The library imports random for itself, but random.choice in the lab file needs its own import. Add it at the top with the other imports.
Returning a 2-tuple instead of a 3-tuple
The caller in CP3 needs the boolean correct flag to increment trivia_correct. Always return three values: destination, distance, correct.
Hardcoding the redirect city
Use function_library.get_redirect(current, distances, dest). It picks a different valid destination from the current city, avoiding the one the player got wrong.

items: trivia_gate defined in lab-4-3.py with a full docstring
Correct answers return the chosen destination plus its true distance
Wrong answers (and invalid answers) return a detour destination from get_redirect
Banners and tone match the mentor quote: detours feel like 'oh well, on we go', not punitive
DEBUG call at the top of main() is removed before moving on

content.body: The integration step. In the in-game loop, the coin-flip Destination Gate block from Lab 4.2 disappears entirely. Replace it with a call to trivia_gate and unpack the 3-tuple return. Increment trivia_asked on every call; increment trivia_correct only when the gate reports a correct answer.

This is where the gate retirement happens. The 12-line coin-flip-then-branch block from 4.2 collapses to about 4 lines. The lab file gets shorter; the interesting logic lives in the lab-local trivia_gate function from CP2.

content.quote: Look at the diff after this CP lands. The in-game loop's gate section becomes four lines: get the wanted destination, run the gate, count the question, count the right answer if any. Twelve lines became four because we moved the decision logic into a function that names what it does. Naming is the cheapest, highest-impact refactor you have access to. Use it constantly.

items: In lab-4-3.py, find the coin-flip Destination Gate block in the in-game loop. It currently has print('Flipping to see if Texas cooperates...') through the print(f'Distance: {distance} miles') line.
Delete every line from print('Flipping...') through the else: branch's destination, distance = function_library.get_redirect(...). The HEADS/TAILS branching is gone entirely.
Keep the line print(f'You're headed to {destination}!') and print(f'Distance: {distance} miles'). They still display the result.

Just before the print statements, add the trivia gate call. Get the wanted destination first: wanted, _ = function_library.get_destination(current_city, distances, choice). Then call destination, distance, correct = trivia_gate(wanted, trivia, current_city, distances).
Increment trivia_asked = trivia_asked + 1 after the gate. If correct: then trivia_correct = trivia_correct + 1.
Run the program and play a full game. Confirm the coin-flip banner is gone, the trivia banner appears, and the game still travels to a destination (correct answer) or a detour (wrong answer).

references.description: Calling a function with multiple arguments. trivia_gate(dest, trivia, current, distances) passes four pieces of state to the function you built in CP2; the function returns one result.

Function returning a tuple, unpacked at the call site. destination, distance, correct = trivia_gate(...) unpacks the 3-tuple into three named variables in one line. Same pattern as rectangle2.py's area, perimeter = compute_dimensions(...).

content.body: Two test paths to walk through: pick Corpus Christi from San Antonio, answer TRUE (correct) and confirm you arrive at Corpus Christi. Start a new game, pick Corpus Christi again, answer FALSE (wrong), and confirm you end up at one of the other San Antonio destinations (Houston, Austin, Laredo, or El Paso).

rows: Leaving the flip_coin() call in
The whole coin-flip section is retired. If the program still flips a coin at the gate, the rewrite did not land. Search the file for flip_coin to confirm.
Forgetting to compute wanted
trivia_gate needs to know the destination the player chose. Call get_destination once to translate the menu choice into a city name, then pass it to trivia_gate.
Incrementing trivia_asked conditionally
Every gate invocation counts as a question asked, regardless of outcome. Increment unconditionally; only trivia_correct is gated on the correct flag.

items: Coin-flip Destination Gate is gone (no flip_coin() at the gate)
trivia_gate is called for every destination choice
trivia_asked and trivia_correct increment correctly
Correct-answer path lands at the chosen destination; wrong-answer path lands at a detour city

content.body: unique_cities = set(visited_cities) as part of per-game state initialization. unique_cities.add(current_city) every time the player arrives somewhere new, including the dead-end return-to-San-Antonio case. This replaces the post-game in-operator loop that built a deduplicated list in Lab 4.2.

Lab 4.2 ended every game with a loop that scanned visited_cities and used if city not in unique_cities: to deduplicate. That works, but it's O(n) per check, O(n²) total, and the code reads like a workaround for the right data structure. set is the right data structure: uniqueness is enforced on insertion, membership is O(1), and the post-game code becomes a single len(unique_cities) call.

content.quote: Notice we are INITIALIZING the set from visited_cities, not just set(). On a fresh game the visited list is just ['San Antonio'] and the set comes out as {'San Antonio'}. On a resumed game, the loaded visited list might have several cities in it, and the set rebuilds with all of them. Both cases handled with one line. That's why set(iterable) exists.

items: In lab-4-3.py, find the per-game state initialization block at the top of the play-again loop. It currently sets current_city, total_miles, etc.

Just after the if not resumed_game: block (the one that resets the per-game counters), add: unique_cities = set(visited_cities). This runs every iteration, including the resume case, and is correct in both: a fresh game starts with a 1-element set, a resume starts with as many elements as the loaded visited_cities list had.

Find every site where visited_cities.append(...) is called. There are three: arrival at a new destination, the dead-end return-to-SA branch, and (transitively) any other spot you've added cities. Mirror each append with unique_cities.add(...) right after it.

Run the program. Play a multi-city game that includes a dead-end retreat (pick Laredo or Texarkana, then choose to return). Confirm the post-game Unique cities visited: count includes the dead-end city even though the player returned.

references.description: set(iterable) takes any iterable and builds a set from its unique elements. Same idea here: build a set from visited_cities.
my_set.add(item) inserts an item; duplicates are silently ignored. This is what makes the dead-end return-to-SA case work without extra checks.

content.body: Run a game where the player visits San Antonio, then Corpus Christi (a beach win). Confirm Unique cities visited: reports 2. Then play a game where the player visits SA, Laredo (a dead end), and returns to SA, then quits. Confirm the count is 2, not 3 (SA and Laredo are the unique ones; the return-to-SA doesn't double-count).

rows: Initializing with set() instead of set(visited_cities)
Resume case breaks. A resumed game has cities in visited_cities already; the set must rebuild from them, not start empty.
Forgetting one of the .add() sites
Easiest to find them all by searching for visited_cities.append. Every append site needs a mirroring add. There are usually 2 or 3.
Putting the set initialization inside if not resumed_game:

Then it never runs on a resumed game, and unique_cities is undefined. The set init runs every iteration of the play-again loop; the if not resumed_game: guard is only for the counters that should reset.

items: unique_cities = set(visited_cities) initialized at the top of every play-again iteration
unique_cities.add(current_city) mirrors every visited_cities.append(...) site
Beach win after SA -> Corpus Christi reports 2 unique cities
Dead-end retreat (SA -> Laredo -> SA -> quit) reports 2 unique cities

content.body: unique_hazards = set(hazards_list) as part of per-game state initialization, and unique_hazards.add(hazard_name) in the hazard branch of the D20 loop. Same pattern as CP4, applied to hazards.

hazards_list records every hazard encounter, including duplicates (if the player faces Buc-ee's three times, the list has three entries). For the trip report's HAZARDS section, the interesting question isn't how many encounters there were (that's total_hazards) but which distinct hazards appeared. Sets handle that uniqueness automatically.

items: In the per-game state init block, right next to where you added unique_cities, add: unique_hazards = set(hazards_list).
In the D20 hazard branch (where hazards_list.append(hazard_name) already runs), add unique_hazards.add(hazard_name) right after it.
Run the program. Play a game with multiple turns so you're likely to face several hazards. Confirm no crashes.

references.description: .add() is idempotent — calling it twice with the same value still results in one entry. This is what gives the trip report its uniqueness for free.

content.body: Play a long game (pick El Paso from SA — 550 miles, lots of D20 turns, lots of hazard chances). The hazards section of the trip report should show fewer entries than Hazards Faced: when the same hazard fires more than once.

rows: Only adding to unique_hazards on escape
Both outcomes count. The hazard appeared, period; whether the player escaped is tracked separately in total_escaped. Add to the set regardless.
Initializing with set() instead of set(hazards_list)
Same issue as CP4. A resumed game has hazards_list populated; the set must rebuild from it.

items: unique_hazards = set(hazards_list) initialized every play-again iteration
unique_hazards.add(name) mirrors the hazards_list.append(name) call
Long games show fewer unique hazards than total encounters when duplicates fire

content.body: Replace the post-game 'in' operator uniqueness loop (for cities) and the hazards_list.copy(); .sort() pattern (for hazards) with sorted() calls on the sets you built in CP4 and CP5. The trip report's HAZARDS section now reads HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (Unique, A-Z).

Sets are unordered. sorted(my_set) returns a new list with the elements in alphabetical order. This replaces two earlier patterns: the Lab 4.2 hazard sort (.copy().sort()) and the Lab 4.2 city uniqueness loop (if city not in unique_cities:). The cleanup is significant: about 8 lines of bookkeeping become 2.

content.quote: sorted() is a great general utility. It works on lists, on sets, on tuples, on anything iterable. It always returns a new list, so you don't have to think about whether to .copy() first. When you see the .copy().sort() pattern in your own code, that's usually a sign sorted() would be cleaner.

items: In lab-4-3.py, find the post-game trip report block (right before the on-screen FINAL TRIP REPORT printout).
Delete the sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy(); sorted_hazards.sort() two-liner.
Delete the unique_cities = []; for city in visited_cities: if city not in unique_cities: unique_cities.append(city) loop.
Add two new one-liners: sorted_unique_cities = sorted(unique_cities) and sorted_unique_hazards = sorted(unique_hazards).
Update the on-screen HAZARDS display to use sorted_unique_hazards and the title 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (Unique, A-Z)'.
Update the save_trip_report call to pass sorted_unique_cities and sorted_unique_hazards in place of the old variables.

references.description: sorted(my_set) returns a sorted list of the set's elements.
Compare against the .copy().sort() pattern this is replacing. Both produce the same end result; sorted() is cleaner and works on any iterable.

content.body: Play a game with at least two distinct hazards. Confirm the on-screen HAZARDS section reads HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (Unique, A-Z) and lists each hazard exactly once, alphabetically.

rows: Calling .sort() on a set
Sets are unordered and have no .sort() method. Use sorted(my_set), which returns a new list.
Forgetting to update the on-screen banner
The title 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (A-Z)' becomes 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (Unique, A-Z)' — the 'Unique' word matters because each hazard now appears at most once.

items: .copy().sort() pattern is gone for hazards
in-operator uniqueness loop is gone for cities
Two sorted(set) calls produce the display lists
On-screen banner reads HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (Unique, A-Z)

content.body: Extend the trip report with a TRIVIA section. Both the on-screen report and trip_report.txt grow a new block right after STATISTICS that shows Questions Asked, Answered Correct, and (when asked > 0) Accuracy as a percentage. save_trip_report's signature grows by two parameters (trivia_asked, trivia_correct).

QA pass. The trip report is the player's takeaway from the run. STATISTICS covered the mileage and hazard escape rate; TRIVIA closes the loop on the new gate mechanic. Without it, the player has no record of how their trivia performance shaped the trip.

content.quote: The trip report and trip_report.txt have to be byte-identical where they overlap. If the TRIVIA section shows up on screen but not in the file, or vice versa, that's a regression I'll flag. Update both code paths in the same pass. And when accuracy is 100%, write 100.0%, not 100%; consistency matters.

items: In lab-4-3.py, in the per-game state init block, add trivia_asked = 0 and trivia_correct = 0 alongside the other resettable counters. Note that these are session-only and reset on resume; a comment near the init can make this explicit.
Extend the save_trip_report function signature to accept trivia_asked and trivia_correct as new parameters (between sorted_hazards and status).

Inside save_trip_report, after the STATISTICS block, write a new TRIVIA block: 'TRIVIA:', then f' Questions Asked: {trivia_asked}', f' Answered Correct: {trivia_correct}', and a conditional f' Accuracy: {accuracy:.1f}%' when trivia_asked > 0.
Mirror the same TRIVIA block in the on-screen report between the STATISTICS block and the closing ===== banner.
Update the call site to pass the two new arguments: save_trip_report(..., trivia_asked, trivia_correct, status).
Update the docstring on save_trip_report to describe the new parameters.

references.description: f'{value:.1f}%' formats a float with one decimal place and a percent suffix. Same format used for the existing Escape Rate display.

content.body: Play a beach-win game with one correct trivia answer. Confirm both the on-screen TRIVIA section and trip_report.txt's TRIVIA block show Questions Asked: 1, Answered Correct: 1, Accuracy: 100.0%. Play a second game with one wrong answer. Confirm accuracy reads 0.0%.

rows: Updating only the on-screen report, not trip_report.txt
Both code paths need the new TRIVIA block. They are separate writes; keep them in sync.
Crashing when trivia_asked is 0
The accuracy line is conditional on trivia_asked > 0 to avoid a division-by-zero. Don't always print it.
Mixing up the parameter order in save_trip_report
Order matters for positional arguments. New parameters go between sorted_hazards and status. The call site has to match the signature.

items: On-screen TRIVIA section shows Asked, Correct, and conditionally Accuracy
trip_report.txt matches the on-screen TRIVIA section
Accuracy displays as 100.0% for one-for-one, not 100%
Accuracy line is skipped entirely when the player faced no trivia gates (impossible in normal play, but defensive)

beforeYouSubmit: function_library.py gained one function (load_trivia); no other library changes this unit
lab-4-3.py defines seven module-level constants including the new TRIVIA_FILE
trivia_gate is defined in lab-4-3.py (lab-local), NOT in the library
main() loads four data files at startup; the load banner reports 10 cities, 10 info records, 8 hazards, and 20 trivia questions across 10 cities
The coin-flip Destination Gate is gone entirely: no flip_coin() call remains at the gate position
unique_cities and unique_hazards are set() objects, initialized at every game start (including the resume path)
Trip report's HAZARDS section reads HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (Unique, A-Z) and shows each hazard at most once
Trip report includes a TRIVIA section with Questions Asked, Answered Correct, and (when asked > 0) Accuracy %
Wrong trivia answer triggers get_redirect and the player ends up at a different destination
Top-of-file comment block present on both files; docstrings on every function
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Module 4.3 introduces set(), .add(), sorted() on sets, and the dict-keyed-by-city-with-list-of-dicts trivia shape. The City class and inheritance are saved for Module 5.

specific: Chapter 8 string methods carried forward from Lab 4.2 (.upper(), .isdigit(), etc.)
All techniques from Modules 1–3 and Labs 4.1–4.2 carry forward
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Set comprehensions ({x for x in items}) are out of scope. Stick to set() + .add(). Classes, methods, and __init__ are saved for Module 5.

warnings: Do not put trivia_gate in function_library.py. It is lab-local: it knows about the game's specific TRUE/FALSE answer format and the on-screen banners. Library functions stay general; lab functions hold game-specific logic.

Do not invent a fallback for cities without trivia. The data file is the source of truth. If a future data update leaves a city without a trivia entry, you want the error to surface, not silently skip the gate.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Save lab-4-3.py as lab-4-3-backup.py before starting Module 5. Lab 5.1 starts by copying 4.3 forward and refactoring the city data into a City class.

ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Copy + Load Trivia). The data file must be in the same folder as lab-4-3.py. CodeGrade provides it as a fixture.
Check CP1. load_trivia is grouping by city but the dictionary lookup is happening with the wrong key. Verify trivia[dest] uses the exact city-name string.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: The coin-flip Destination Gate banner still prints during play
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Wire the Trivia Gate). The old gate code wasn't replaced, only commented out, or a duplicate copy remained. Delete the flip_coin() + HEADS/TAILS branching entirely.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip report shows Unique cities visited: 0 even after a multi-city game
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 (Sets - Unique Cities). The set is being created but .add() is never called, OR the set is being reinitialized inside the in-game loop instead of once per game.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: HAZARDS section shows duplicate entries (e.g., 'Buc-ee\'s appears!' twice)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP5 + CP6. Either the post-game display is reading hazards_list (which has duplicates) instead of unique_hazards (a set), or the sort is happening on the wrong source.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Wrong trivia answer produces 'CORRECT! You earned your destination.'
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2. The comparison in trivia_gate is reversed: confirm if player_answer == q['answer']: branches to the correct-answer message, not the wrong-answer one.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: TRIVIA section shows Accuracy: 0.0% after every correct answer
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP7. trivia_correct is initialized but never incremented. Verify the post-trivia_gate code does if correct: trivia_correct = trivia_correct + 1.

ifYouGetStuck: Test load_trivia in isolation before wiring it in. Run python3 function_library.py with a DEBUG block. You should see 20 questions across 10 cities

Test trivia_gate in isolation by adding a temporary call at the top of main() before the main menu loop. Pass a hardcoded destination and confirm both the correct-answer path and the wrong-answer path behave
If your trip report mixes the new TRIVIA section into the wrong place, look at the order of the screen-print block. STATISTICS comes before TRIVIA, and TRIVIA comes before the final ===== banner
Set membership and uniqueness: s = set(); s.add('x'); s.add('x'); len(s) is 1. This is the property you're leaning on for unique_cities and unique_hazards
Post in #labs on Discord with your specific error message. Include the line number. Anjali monitors the channel and surfaces patterns to the instructor
